narrative:
President Donald Trump announced Friday that he was directing all federal agencies to "immediately cease" use of Anthropic's AI tools, following weeks of disputes between the AI company and Pentagon officials over military applications of artificial intelligence [1]. The conflict centers on the Department of Defense's attempt to modify terms of existing AI contracts to eliminate restrictions on military deployment and instead permit "all lawful use" of the technology [1].

## Background and Contract Details

Anthropic was the first major AI laboratory to partner with the US military through a $200 million deal signed with the Pentagon in July 2025 [1]. The company created custom models called "Claude Gov" with fewer restrictions than standard versions, and is currently the only AI company working with classified military systems [1]. The AI tools are deployed through platforms provided by Palantir and Amazon's cloud services for classified work [1].

Current applications of Claude Gov include routine tasks like report writing and document summarization, as well as intelligence analysis and military planning, according to an anonymous source familiar with the situation [1].

## The Dispute

The Pentagon has pressured Anthropic and other AI companies to modify contract terms established last July, seeking to remove existing restrictions on AI deployment [1]. Anthropic has resisted these changes, arguing they could enable AI use in fully autonomous lethal weapons systems or mass surveillance of US citizens [1]. While the Pentagon states it does not currently use AI for these purposes and has no plans to do so, Trump administration officials have opposed allowing civilian tech companies to dictate military use of critical technologies [1].

## Trump's Response and Timeline

Trump announced the ban in a Truth Social post, stating: "The Leftwing nut jobs at Anthropic have made a DISASTROUS MISTAKE trying to STRONG-ARM the Department of War" [1]. The directive includes a six-month phase-out period for agencies currently using Anthropic's tools, potentially allowing time for further negotiations [1].

## Industry Context

The dispute reflects broader tensions as Silicon Valley has shifted from largely avoiding defense work to increasingly embracing military contracts [1]. Google, OpenAI, and xAI signed similar Pentagon deals around the same time as Anthropic, though only Anthropic currently works with classified systems [1]. This week, several hundred workers from OpenAI and Google signed an open letter supporting Anthropic's position and criticizing their own companies' decisions to remove usage restrictions [1].

citations.title: Trump moves to ban Anthropic from the US government
citations.quote: US President Donald Trump announced Friday that he was instructing every federal agency to 'immediately cease' use of Anthropic's AI tools. The move comes after Anthropic and top officials clashed for weeks over military applications of artificial intelligence.

keyDevelopments: Trump announces immediate ban on federal use of Anthropic AI tools
Six-month phase-out period established for current government users
Pentagon seeks to eliminate AI usage restrictions in existing contracts
Anthropic resists changes, citing concerns over autonomous weapons and surveillance
Hundreds of tech workers from competing companies sign letter supporting Anthropic
Dispute tests limits of Silicon Valley's growing embrace of defense contracts
